user: For each feature, define acceptance criteria and target metrics we'd consider a successful launch. | assistant: Below are concise acceptance criteria and target metrics for Feature A and Feature B. Each section covers functional acceptance, non‑functional (performance/security/ops), instrumentation/measurement, rollout strategy and rollback gates, plus post‑launch success targets measured at 30/60/90 days.

Feature A — Acceptance criteria & success targets
Purpose summary (for context): a priority feature tied to early retention and conversion; must be stable for M6 launch and support marketing amplification.

Functional acceptance
- All core user stories completed and validated against acceptance criteria.  
- End‑to‑end happy path works in production (signup → use → expected outcome) with no manual steps required.  
- UI/UX implemented per design sign‑off; accessibility basics met (keyboard nav + alt text).  
- Feature flagging in place to toggle exposure by cohort.  
- Beta with at least one internal and one external user cohort completed (≥20 external beta users) and feedback triaged.

Instrumentation & analytics
- Events instrumented for key touchpoints (impression, click/attempt, success, failure, conversion/monetization event).  
- Events validated end‑to‑end into analytics/warehouse; dashboards show these metrics within 24 hours.  
- Experiment wiring (if running A/B) or exposure logging for attribution is in place.

Non‑functional & compliance
- Automated tests covering regressions for critical flows; acceptance tests green in CI.  
- Performance: p95 API/UI latency within acceptable bounds (target p95 < 500ms for critical calls).  
- Error budget: observed error rate (5xx/JS errors) < 0.5% post‑launch.  
- Uptime/SLA: no production downtime > 1 hour attributable to feature rollout in first 30 days.  
- Security: no open high/critical vulnerabilities; security review completed.  
- Privacy/compliance: any data collected respects consent/opt‑out rules and legal sign‑off obtained where needed.

Operational readiness
- Runbooks for launch incidents and rollback documented and distributed.  
- Monitoring & alerts configured (error spikes, latency, adoption anomalies).  
- CS/Support: Knowledge base article and canned responses live; support triage rules updated.  
- Billing integration (if monetized): pricing & billing flows tested in staging and live sandbox.

Rollout plan & rollback gates
- Staged rollout plan (example): internal (100% devs) → 5% production users (1 week) → 25% (2 weeks) → 100%, with metrics review at each gate.  
- Automatic rollback/stop criteria at any stage if:
  - Error rate > 1% sustained for 30 minutes, OR
  - p95 latency > 1s sustained for 30 minutes, OR
  - Critical business KPIs degrade (trial→paid conversion drop >10% vs control) within cohort window.

Target launch metrics (success thresholds)
- Adoption: ≥30% of active users engage with Feature A at least once within 60 days of exposure.  
- Quality: ≤3 P1/P0 production issues in the first 30 days post‑launch.  
- Conversion impact: trial→paid conversion lifts by ≥+5 percentage points for exposed cohorts within 60 days (or alternative agreed conversion target).  
- Engagement: average session time or usage frequency for adopters grows by ≥10% vs baseline.  
- Support: number of billing/support tickets related to the feature ≤ baseline + 10% during first 30 days (with resolution SLA met).  
- Evaluation windows: primary evaluation at 30 days for stability and 60 days for adoption/conversion; 90‑day deep impact review.

Feature B — Acceptance criteria & success targets
Purpose summary: second major feature intended to drive acquisition/monetization and longer‑term retention; must be production‑ready and monetization/billing‑integrated by M11.

Functional acceptance
- All prioritized user stories for the launch MVP are implemented and pass acceptance tests.  
- Integrations with any third‑party services (payments, partners, APIs) are end‑to‑end tested.  
- UI/UX sign‑off complete; localization or region‑specific behavior handled if applicable.  
- Feature flag + cohort rollout controls available.

Instrumentation & analytics
- Comprehensive event coverage for features, conversion, and downstream usage (including revenue events).  
- Attribution is configured so revenue and user flows can be tied to exposure to Feature B.  
- Dashboards and experiment reports available within 24–48 hours.

Non‑functional & compliance
- Automated regression and integration tests included in CI; feature passes pre‑release test suites.  
- Performance: target p95 latency < 500–700ms for critical APIs depending on complexity; stress tests show capacity for expected launch load with margin.  
- Error rate: post‑launch error rate < 0.5% for feature‑specific flows.  
- Security & regulatory: where billing or PII is involved, PCI/data privacy checks complete and documented.  
- Monitoring & alerts set for business and technical KPIs.

Operational readiness
- Billing/pricing changes validated in sandbox and test customers; invoices and refunds flows tested.  
- CS/Support ready with KB, escalation path, and training.  
- Partner/marketing playbook ready for launch coordination.

Rollout plan & rollback gates
- Staged rollout example: internal → 1–5% beta (selected power users) → 25% → 100% with KPI gates at each stage.  
- Rollback/stop triggers:
  - Error rate > 1% sustained, OR  
  - p95 latency > 1s sustained, OR  
  - Material negative revenue signal (e.g., increased churn among exposed cohort >2 percentage points) OR  
  - Customer complaints/ticket surge beyond predefined threshold.

Target launch metrics (success thresholds)
- Adoption: ≥25–35% of the target cohort (or active users) engage with Feature B at least once within 60 days of exposure.  
- Monetization: achieve ≥20% lift in new MRR attributable to Feature B marketing + product within 30–60 days post‑launch (or meet a pre‑agreed revenue target).  
- Conversion: ≥35% of trial users exposed to Feature B convert to active users of B within 60 days (if applicable).  
- Retention lift: for users who adopt Feature B, improve 90‑day retention by ≥10% vs comparable cohort.  
- Stability: ≤2 P1/P0 issues in first 30 days post‑launch.  
- Evaluation windows: 30 days (stability), 60 days (adoption & revenue impact), 90 days (retention & unit economics).

General acceptance & measurement rules (applies to both features)
- Owners must produce a launch readiness checklist with sign‑offs from Product, Engineering, QA, Security, Ops, Marketing, and CS before widening rollout.  
- All feature metrics must have a clear baseline and control cohorts (for attribution and A/B tests) defined pre‑launch.  
- Post‑launch review at 30/60/90 days with documented lessons, clear go/no‑go decisions for expansion of marketing spend, and next‑step backlog items.  
- If KPI thresholds are not met within the agreed evaluation windows, owners will propose corrective actions (optimizations, re‑scoping, or feature rollbacks) and an updated timeline.

If you want, I can convert these criteria into a launch checklist template with binary sign‑offs per item and a recommended gating cadence for staged rollouts.

user: If engineering bandwidth becomes constrained, which parts of feature A can be scoped down to an MVP and which are must-have? | assistant: Below is a prioritized breakdown of Feature A into must‑have vs scopiable items if engineering bandwidth is constrained, plus a recommended minimal rollout approach and gating rules to protect quality and measurement.

Must‑have (do not cut)
- Core user flow (happy path) end‑to‑end
  - Fully working minimal workflow that delivers the feature’s primary user value (signup → use → success).
- Stable backend API implementing required business logic
  - Necessary validation, persistence, and any consistency guarantees required for correct behavior.
- Authentication & authorization integration
  - Correct user identity handling and permission checks for any feature data.
- Basic frontend UI to access and use the feature
  - Clean, usable UI for core tasks (no advanced polish required).
- Instrumentation for key metrics
  - Events for exposure, core actions, success/failure, and conversion so you can measure adoption and impact.
- Feature flags and staged rollout capability
  - Ability to gate the feature per cohort and roll back quickly.
- Basic testing & CI gating
  - Unit/integration tests for critical paths and automated checks in CI to prevent regressions.
- Monitoring, alerts, and runbook
  - Error/latency monitoring, alert thresholds, and an incident runbook for launch.
- Support readiness
  - One KB article / canned support responses and CS briefing for the MVP behavior.
- Privacy/compliance signoff for any data collected
  - Ensure consent/opt‑out and any regulatory checks are met.

Should‑have (important but can be deferred to post‑MVP)
- Full edge‑case handling and complex validation flows
  - E.g., every rare error case, complex retry logic — handle core failure modes first.
- Advanced UX polish & micro‑interactions
  - Animations, extra UX refinements, or multi‑step wizard niceties.
- Extensive cross‑browser/device polishing beyond supported baseline
  - Support target browsers/devices; defer exhaustive compatibility matrix work.
- Rich analytics/attribution slices
  - Basic events must exist; defer granular dashboards, long‑tail analytics, and advanced cohort modeling.
- Deep performance optimizations
  - Basic acceptable latency must be met; advanced caching and micro‑optimizations can come later unless required for scale.
- Multi‑language / full localization
  - Launch in primary language(s); defer full i18n work.
- Comprehensive accessibility beyond baseline
  - Meet key a11y requirements (keyboard, labels); defer advanced a11y audit fixes if not critical.
- Admin/ops UI for advanced controls
  - Admin tools for reporting or complex config can be postponed if feature flags suffice.

Nice‑to‑have (defer)
- Personalization tied to the feature (ML or complex rules)
- Rich export/reporting or heavy integration partners
- Bulk import/export or advanced migration tooling
- In‑depth onboarding flows specific to feature (beyond basic guidance)
- Full automation of any secondary billing or monetization flows (if not required at launch)

Minimal viable rollout approach (recommended)
1. Define the MVP scope strictly around the core user value and the must‑have list above.  
2. Implement with feature flags and plan a staged rollout (internal → small beta cohort → broader).  
3. Instrument everything minimal but sufficient for attribution and decisioning (you must know if it moves conversion/retention).  
4. Reserve the pre‑launch stabilization sprint (30–40% buffer capacity) and a small squad on‑call during the initial rollout window.  
5. Triage and iterate: collect metrics in first 30/60 days and push high‑impact “should‑have” improvements first.

Gating rules for proceeding beyond MVP
- Only expand rollout if:
  - Error rate for the feature < predefined threshold (e.g., <0.5% feature‑specific errors) and
  - Performance targets met for critical APIs (p95 within acceptable bound) and
  - Early adoption metrics show expected baseline engagement (e.g., X% of exposed cohort uses it once within 14 days) or marketing funnel intact.
- If any gate fails, pause expansion, roll back if severe, and prioritize fixes over feature expansions.

Impact tradeoffs to communicate to stakeholders
- Cutting should‑haves reduces polish and secondary UX flows but preserves time to market and measurement capability.  
- Deferring analytics detail slows ability to deep‑diagnose long‑tail problems but does not prevent initial go/no‑go decisions if core instrumentation exists.  
- Postponing personalization and integrations reduces potential uplift but lowers engineering risk and launch complexity.

If you want, I can convert this into a one‑page MVP checklist (binary sign‑offs) that maps directly to your sprint plan and assigns owners for each must‑have item.

user: For marketing experiments in Q2, propose three channel tests and the minimum statistical thresholds we'd need to continue funding them. | assistant: Here are three practical Q2 channel experiments (aligned to your M4 ramp), each with the primary KPI, test design, minimum statistical thresholds to continue funding, and recommended runtime/stop rules.

1) Paid Search: keyword/landing-page test
- What to test: high‑intent keyword match types or keyword-group A vs B, each driving to a matched landing‑page variant (control = current best performer).  
- Primary KPI: trial signups per click (or trial CPA). Secondary: trial→paid conversion.  
- Minimum thresholds to continue funding:
  - Relative uplift: ≥10% improvement in signup conversion rate OR ≥15% reduction in CPA vs control.  
  - Statistical: p ≤ 0.05 (two‑tailed) with power ≈ 80%.  
  - Minimum sample: ~100 conversions per variant (so ~200 total) — if baseline conversion rate is low, compute sample size for detectable relative lift (use standard A/B sample calculators).  
- Runtime & stop rules:
  - Run at least 2 weeks and until min sample is reached (whichever is longer).  
  - Stop early if the variant is significantly worse (p ≤ 0.05 and >10% worse) for 48–72 hours or if CPA exceeds acceptable limit for >72 hours.  
- Continue funding if threshold met and LTV:CAC or first‑30‑day revenue metrics are stable/improving.

2) Paid Social: creative × audience matrix (prospecting)
- What to test: 2–3 creative concepts × 2 audience segments (e.g., lookalike vs interest-based). Use multiple ads per cell for creative rotation.  
- Primary KPI: cost per qualified lead (or trial signup). Secondary: CTR and landing-page CVR.  
- Minimum thresholds to continue funding:
  - Relative uplift: ≥15% increase in qualified signups or ≥15% lower CPL vs control cohort.  
  - Statistical: p ≤ 0.05 with ~80% power.  
  - Minimum sample: ~150 conversions per tested cell (or aggregate at audience level if creatives are pooled). If resource-limited, require at least 100 conversions per high-level audience test.  
- Runtime & stop rules:
  - Run at least 3 weeks to let audiences stabilize and creative frequency normalize.  
  - Pause cells showing clear underperformance (p ≤ 0.05 and >15% worse) after 3–5 days of stable data or once minimum adverse sample accrued.  
- Continue funding if the winning audience/creative delivers sustainable CPA improvements and shows acceptable trial→paid conversion downstream.

3) Email/onboarding nurture: triggered behavioral flow vs baseline drip
- What to test: targeted behavioral sequences (e.g., feature‑use triggers + personalized CTAs) vs standard generic nurture sequence. Randomize at signup into control vs experiment.  
- Primary KPI: trial→paid conversion (within 30 days). Secondary: Day‑7 activation and engagement with onboarding checklist.  
- Minimum thresholds to continue funding:
  - Relative uplift: ≥10% lift in trial→paid conversion or ≥15% lift in Day‑7 activation.  
  - Statistical: p ≤ 0.05 (two‑tailed), power ≈ 80%.  
  - Minimum sample: at least 200 recipients per arm and ≥50 conversion events total across arms (adjust sample based on baseline conversion to detect 10% relative lift).  
- Runtime & stop rules:
  - Run at least 4 weeks to capture downstream conversions (30‑day window).  
  - Stop early if the experiment causes a statistically significant drop in conversion or a measurable rise in unsubscribe/complaints.  
- Continue funding if conversion uplift is sustained and cost (email tool + creative effort) per incremental paid conversion is acceptable relative to CAC targets.

General experiment governance (applies to all tests)
- Predefine baselines and the metric calculation (attribution windows, deduping, cohort definitions) before starting.  
- Holdout/backtest control population must be consistent and large enough to be statistically valid.  
- Use one primary KPI per experiment to avoid multiple-testing mistakes; treat other metrics as secondary signals.  
- Minimum budget allocation: reserve ~10–15% of the performance budget for exploratory tests; scale winners incrementally (don’t instantly replace all spend).  
- Escalation/kill rules: enforce stop rules for negative business impact (e.g., conversion drop >10% with p ≤ 0.05) and for unacceptable CAC override thresholds.

If you want, I can compute exact sample sizes for each test given your current baseline conversion rates and typical daily traffic, or produce a simple experiment tracker template to run these tests.

user: On operations automation, rank the manual processes by cost and frequency and recommend which to automate first for fastest ROI. | assistant: Below I rank common manual operational processes by combined estimated cost (dollars per hour of labor) and frequency (how often the work recurs), give approximate monthly FTE‑hour ranges (based on your scale assumptions), and recommend which to automate first to achieve the fastest ROI.

Assumptions
- Fully‑loaded labor cost ≈ $80/hr (used only to give a sense of dollar impact).  
- “Frequency” = how often tasks repeat (daily/weekly/monthly) and how many tickets/items per period.  
- Hours are conservative ranges; please replace with your actual measured hours for precise ROI.

Ranked list (highest combined impact → lowest)

1) Support triage & first‑response (routing, templated replies, simple troubleshooting)
- Frequency: Very high (daily, high ticket volume).  
- Estimated monthly hours manual today: 120–240 hrs/month.  
- Estimated monthly labor cost impact: $9.6k–$19.2k.  
- Why high impact: repetitive, high-volume work that can be partially automated (routing + templated responses + chatbots), freeing CS and engineering time.  
- Automation ease: Medium (rules + integrations + light bot); quick win possible.  
- Recommendation: Automate first if you want the fastest ROI. (Fast payback; largest recurring hours savings.)

2) Billing, invoicing & collections reconciliation (including dunning)
- Frequency: High (monthly billing cycles; ongoing exceptions).  
- Estimated monthly hours manual today: 80–120 hrs/month.  
- Estimated monthly labor cost impact: $6.4k–$9.6k; plus recovered revenue (0.5–2% MRR typical).  
- Why high impact: directly improves cash flow and recovers lost revenue; reduces disputes and manual accounting work.  
- Automation ease: Medium (requires integration with PSP, accounting, and reconciliation logic).  
- Recommendation: Automate in parallel with support triage or immediately after — high ROI and financially material.

3) Manual onboarding & account provisioning (manual setup, handoffs, entitlement changes)
- Frequency: High when acquisition ramps (daily for new customers).  
- Estimated monthly hours manual today: 60–120 hrs/month.  
- Estimated monthly labor cost impact: $4.8k–$9.6k.  
- Why impact: delays and manual errors increase time‑to‑value and churn; automation improves retention and scales growth.  
- Automation ease: Medium (mix of product changes, automation scripts, and integrations).  
- Recommendation: Prioritize soon after support/billing if onboarding friction is tied to early churn.

4) Manual reporting & recurring analytics pulls (ad‑hoc dashboards, periodic reports)
- Frequency: High (weekly/monthly management reporting).  
- Estimated monthly hours manual today: 40–80 hrs/month.  
- Estimated monthly labor cost impact: $3.2k–$6.4k.  
- Why impact: time drains for Ops/Product; automation improves decision speed and reduces errors.  
- Automation ease: Low–Medium (BI tool + ETL feed usually sufficient).  
- Recommendation: Automate early—small engineering/ops effort with quick payoff; improves experiment cadence.

5) Refunds & disputes processing (payment disputes, manual refunds)
- Frequency: Medium (depends on churn/complaints).  
- Estimated monthly hours manual today: 20–60 hrs/month.  
- Estimated monthly labor cost impact: $1.6k–$4.8k + direct revenue impact.  
- Why impact: directly affects cash and customer sentiment; recurring but lower volume than billing reconciliation.  
- Automation ease: Medium (workflows + integrations with PSP & CRM).  
- Recommendation: Automate once billing is automated, or include as part of billing automation scope.

6) Manual deployments, release orchestration & incident runbook execution
- Frequency: Low–Medium (per release and incidents).  
- Estimated monthly hours manual today: 40–100 hrs/month (concentrated around releases or incidents).  
- Estimated monthly labor cost impact: $3.2k–$8.0k.  
- Why impact: high cost per event (on‑call, developer time), can cause outsized revenue/regret if incidents occur.  
- Automation ease: Medium–High (CI/CD and scripted runbooks).  
- Recommendation: Automate as a risk mitigation step prior to major launches (tie to Feature A/B schedules).

7) CRM/data entry and partner onboarding admin
- Frequency: High for entry tasks but low per‑item impact.  
- Estimated monthly hours manual today: 40–160 hrs/month (varies by sales & partnerships activity).  
- Estimated monthly labor cost impact: $3.2k–$12.8k (but lower ROI per hour automated).  
- Why impact: important for sales efficiency, but automating gives incremental benefits.  
- Automation ease: Low (many SaaS tools and Zapier-style integrations can reduce time).  
- Recommendation: Automate after higher-dollar, higher-frequency processes — use lightweight integrations.

8) Complex ad‑hoc operational workflows (legal admin, custom contract handling)
- Frequency: Low (occasional, high complexity).  
- Estimated monthly hours manual today: 10–40 hrs/month.  
- Estimated monthly labor cost impact: $0.8k–$3.2k.  
- Why impact: lower recurring ROI from automation because tasks are often one‑off and need human judgment.  
- Automation ease: Low (requires careful templating + approvals).  
- Recommendation: Defer or template partial automation where sensible; keep human in loop for exceptions.

Recommendation for fastest ROI (practical sequencing)
1. Start with support triage & response automation (highest recurring hours saved; fastest payback).  
2. In parallel (or immediately after), automate billing/invoicing & dunning (direct cash recovery + labor savings).  
3. Next wave: automate onboarding/provisioning and recurring reporting/analytics (improves retention and decisioning).  
4. Then, implement CI/CD + runbook automation to lower incident cost and support safer launches.  
5. Follow with refunds/workflow automation and CRM/partner integration automations.  
6. Defer complex legal/one‑off process automation unless volume increases.

Quick implementation guidance to accelerate ROI
- Measure baseline: log actual hours for each process for 2–4 weeks to refine ROI calculations.  
- Start with a pilot: target one high‑volume queue or one billing cohort to validate automation approach.  
- Use a mix: prefer rules + SaaS tools (Zendesk macros, payment‑processor webhooks, Zapier/Workato) for fast wins; reserve engineering for integrations and durable automations.  
- Build monitoring: track hours saved, ticket resolution time, recovered revenue, and customer satisfaction to validate ROI.  
- Iterate: expand automation scope only after pilots show improved KPIs.

If you want, I can convert this into a prioritized automation roadmap with estimated project effort (hours), owners, and expected payback timelines consistent with your sprint plan.

user: Create a contingency playbook for hire delays or early departures that preserves launch dates as much as possible. | assistant: Below is a pragmatic contingency playbook you can use if hires are delayed or someone leaves early. It’s organized by triggers, immediate actions, short/medium/long responses, scope & schedule levers to protect launch dates (Feature A M6, Feature B M11), and checklists for handoffs and contractor onboarding. Use the decision thresholds to escalate appropriately.

Summary principle
- Preserve the launch date where feasible by: (1) triaging scope to the MVP must‑haves, (2) rapidly plugging capacity gaps with contractors or internal reallocation, and (3) protecting buffer sprints and CI/CD/monitoring investments that reduce launch risk.

Triggers & thresholds (use these to pick a response level)
- Minor delay / <2 weeks: internal reallocation + temporary reprioritization.  
- Moderate delay / 2–6 weeks or single early departure: hire contractor(s) / fractional resource + aggressive onboarding; freeze non‑critical work.  
- Major delay / >6 weeks or multiple departures: trigger scoped MVP + adjust marketing ramp and consider launch contingency (defer nonessential launches/features).

Immediate actions (within 48 hours)
1. Incident standup
   - Convene PM, Eng lead, Design lead, Recruitment, and Ops within 24–48 hrs to assess impact and set decisions.  
2. Announce status & single source of truth
   - Communicate to stakeholders: who left/delayed, projected delay window, immediate mitigation plan owner.  
3. Freeze new scope
   - Stop adding new work; triage current sprint for critical path items only.  
4. Reassign owners for any in‑flight tasks of the departing role
   - Temporary owners identified and accountable for next 7–14 days.  
5. Start rapid hiring actions
   - Recruiter posts, shortlist contractors, and alert hiring panel; identify budget from contingency (10%) if needed.

Short-term play (1–2 weeks)
1. Internal reallocation & pairing
   - Pair remaining engineers with PM/Designer to continue critical paths. Use BE1 + FE pair programming for FE/back-end gaps where possible.  
2. Bring on contractors quickly
   - Engage one senior contractor (backend/frontend/DevOps/QA) for 4–8 week blocks. Focus them on the must‑have items. Use vetted marketplaces or existing supplier relationships for speed.  
3. Enforce MVP scope
   - Apply the “must‑have” list for Feature A (core flow, instrumentation, flags, CI, monitoring). Defer should‑haves and nice‑to‑haves.  
4. Adjust sprint commitments
   - Reduce planned velocity to ≤80% for the next sprint to preserve buffer for unexpected churn. Reserve the stabilization sprint (sprint 12) fully for launch readiness.  
5. Update marketing & ops
   - If staffing affects integrations or landing pages, pause scaling media spend until feature flag and telemetry confirm readiness.

Medium-term play (2–8 weeks)
1. Contractor onboarding & knowledge capture
   - Provide contractors: architecture docs, event schemas, acceptance criteria, runbooks, and a single engineering contact. Use a 2‑week sprint cadence with clear deliverables.  
2. Hire acceleration
   - Use referral bonuses, recruiter intensives, and interview sprints to shorten hire time. Consider flexible offers (start date guaranteed, equity bonuses) if needed.  
3. Scope reduction plan (if capacity still tight)
   - Formalize cut list: drop deferred UX polish, advanced analytics, localization, complex error workflows, admin UIs, and personalization. Keep instrumentation and flags.  
4. Monitoring & safety
   - Prioritize CI/CD, canary deployments, automated tests, and incident runbooks to reduce launch risk even with smaller teams.  
5. Budget & vendor tradeoffs
   - Temporarily shift some work to managed vendors (e.g., hosted analytics setup, QA services) if cost of delay exceeds contractor fees.

Longer-term play (>8 weeks or multiple role losses)
1. Reassess launch dates only as last resort
   - If you cannot staff critical roles within 8–10 weeks despite contractors, prepare a controlled, communicated delay and staging plan (e.g., soft launch with feature flag limited to <25% users).  
2. Reprioritize roadmap & OKRs
   - Move lower-priority OKRs to next quarter and reallocate budget to ensure core revenue/retention objectives are met.  
3. Institutionalize cross-training
   - Build permanent cross-training and documentation standards to reduce single‑person risk going forward.

Staffing alternatives & when to use them
- Short block contractors (2–8 weeks): Use for immediate capacity (fix backlogs, finish integration work). Best for engineers, front‑end, QA, and DevOps. Trigger: gap >2 weeks.  
- Fractional PM/Design (part‑time senior): Useful if PM/Designer unavailable early; trigger for immediate coordination needs.  
- Small dev agency or boutique shop: Consider for parallel execution (build landing pages, run paid social creative integration, or take on non-core features). Trigger: >4‑6 week gap + budget available.  
- Internal pull from other teams: Borrow engineers from adjacent teams or product areas; useful for <2 week gaps or targeted tasks.  
Decision criteria: contractor for short-delivery, agency if you need parallel lanes and QA/PM overhead is manageable, internal pull if impact is small and ramp cost low.

Scope reduction playbook to preserve launches
- Apply immediately: freeze everything not in “must‑have” list (see previous MVP answer).  
- Specific items to cut first:
  - Advanced UX polish & animations
  - Full localization & extended browser support
  - Noncritical analytics slices and long‑tail dashboards
  - Personalization/ML features
  - Admin and ops UI (use manual ops for short term)
  - Complex partner integrations (defer until post‑launch)
- Keep: instrumentation for core metrics, feature flags, CI/CD, monitoring, and a minimal KB for support.

Sprint & release adjustments
- Reduce velocity in the sprint following the staffing change to maintain quality.  
- Maintain the hard buffer sprint immediately before each launch (sprints 12 and 22). If resources are constrained, don’t reallocate this buffer.  
- Use feature flags and a staged rollout to avoid all‑or‑nothing releases; can launch MVP to select cohorts while continuing incremental work.

Communication plan
- Immediate (day 1–2): Notify execs and stakeholders of staffing change, impact assessment, and mitigation owner.  
- Weekly: Status updates on hires/contractors, scope changes, and KPIs for the next launch gate.  
- Pre-launch gate: distribute launch readiness checklist and sign‑offs from Product, Eng, QA, CS, Ops, and Marketing.  
- Customer-facing: if launch risk materializes into delay, prepare templated customer messaging explaining rationale and benefits, with an ETA and compensatory offers if appropriate.

Handoff checklist for early departure (to use when someone gives notice)
- Code & access
  - List of repos owned, credentials (rotate after departure), and admin access transfer completed.  
- Architecture & docs
  - Updated architecture diagram, API contracts, event schema, and an index of runbooks.  
- Current tasks & status
  - Sprint board export, list of in‑progress tickets, PRs, and blockers.  
- Knowledge transfer sessions
  - Two 60–90 minute walkthroughs recording key systems, with Q&A.  
- Contacts & vendors
  - List of external contacts, vendor contract details, and billing contacts.  
- On‑call & runbooks
  - Confirm on‑call transfer and ensure runbooks are updated and accessible.

Rapid contractor onboarding checklist (first 48–72 hrs)
- Accounts & access: repo, CI, staging, analytics, slack, VPN.  
- Architecture brief & 1‑pg summary of critical paths.  
- Acceptance criteria for immediate sprint items and test cases.  
- Single technical contact and sprint cadence.  
- Security & compliance briefing and NDA signed.  
- Short first task that yields a visible deliverable in 3–7 days.

Decision matrix (quick rubric)
- If delay <2 weeks → internal reallocation + temp slowdown in noncritical work.  
- If delay 2–6 weeks → hire contractor(s) for focused deliverables; freeze nonessential features; accelerate hiring.  
- If delay >6 weeks or multiple departures → contractor + scoped MVP + re-evaluate launch date if critical path blocked.

Use of contingency budget
- Reserve contingency (10% of budget) for contractor costs and recruiter acceleration fees. Prioritize spending on roles that unblock launch-critical tasks (senior backend, DevOps, QA).

Checklist to preserve quality under constrained bandwidth
- Never cut instrumentation or feature flags.  
- Maintain CI/CD + automated tests for critical paths.  
- Keep the stabilization buffer sprints intact; do not repurpose them.  
- Limit concurrent launches and marketing spend until launch KPIs and production stability are validated for the MVP.

Final operational tip
- After the incident, run a blameless post‑mortem focused on hiring process, ramp times, documentation gaps, and cross‑training needs; convert findings into mandatory improvements (recruiting SLA, onboarding checklist, documentation quotas) to reduce recurrence.

If you want, I can convert this playbook into a 1‑page checklist per role (engineering, PM, design, recruiting) or map specific contractor profiles and expected hourly rates against the contingency budget to help you act quickly.

user: Assume we can contract a third-party analytics vendor under standard terms - recommend contract length, onboarding timeline, and integration priorities. | assistant: Good plan — below is a practical recommendation you can action quickly: contract length, a phased onboarding timeline aligned to your M4 marketing ramp and M6 Feature A launch, prioritized integrations, success criteria, roles/responsibilities, and sensible contractual protections.

Contract length & structure
- Pilot + option to extend: Start with a 6‑month pilot agreement (limited scope, included implementation hours, lower commitment).  
- Post‑pilot: If pilot meets KPIs, convert to a 12–24 month contract with quarterly business reviews.  
- Terms to insist on in both phases:
  - SLAs for service availability and data delivery latency (see KPIs below).  
  - Professional services / implementation hours included in pilot (fixed amount).  
  - Dedicated onboarding CSM or solutions engineer during pilot.  
  - Data portability & exit clause: guaranteed export (raw event + derived tables) in standard formats (S3/CSV/Parquet) within 30 days of termination.  
  - Security & compliance: SOC2 / ISO / GDPR/PCI assurances as required by your markets.  
  - Performance credits / remediation if SLA missed.  
  - 30–60 day termination notice after pilot or short trial cancellation clause.

Onboarding timeline (recommended, mapped to weeks; start as early as Month 1 or by early Month 2 to meet M4/M6 goals)
- Week 0–2: Contracting & kickoff
  - Sign SOW for pilot scope; align objectives, KPIs, stakeholders, access plan, and implementation owner.  
  - Deliverables: project plan, event taxonomy template, success criteria.
- Week 2–6: Core event ingestion & identity baseline
  - Integrate core SDKs/webhooks or server-side ingestion for primary events (signup, activation steps, conversion, feature usage).  
  - Implement user identity mapping (stable user IDs, device/session stitching, basic CDP joins).  
  - Deliverables: ingest pipeline running to vendor, sample events validated, identity mapping tests passed.
- Week 6–10: Dashboards, funnels & attribution
  - Build production‑ready dashboards (onboarding funnel, activation cohorts, acquisition attribution, basic feature adoption).  
  - Wire attribution connectors for paid channels (ad networks, tracking pixels) and marketing sources.  
  - Deliverables: dashboards accessible to stakeholders, baseline metrics populated, QA sign‑off.
- Week 10–14: Experiment wiring & integrations
  - Integrate with feature flag/experiment system (exposure logging) and A/B frameworks; enable cohort exports to marketing tools / ESPs.  
  - Add near‑real‑time feeds if needed (webhooks or streaming) for retargeting and automation triggers.  
  - Deliverables: experiment attribution validated; one end‑to‑end experiment traceable.
- Week 14–18 (optional/phase 2): Advanced capabilities
  - Real‑time segments / API for personalization, advanced lifetime/value modeling, predictive features if vendor supports.  
  - Deliverables: real‑time segment API or export, test personalization flow.  
- Week 18–24: Optimization & handover
  - Implement additional dashboards, automated alerts, training, runbooks, and handover to internal analytics owner.  
  - Deliverables: documented pipelines, runbooks, trained users, acceptance sign‑off, go/no‑go decision on conversion to long‑term contract.

Integration priorities (ranked order)
1. Event taxonomy & instrumentation
  - Agree on canonical event schema, naming conventions, and required properties for each key action (signup, activation step IDs, feature events, conversion/revenue events). This is the foundational first step.
2. User identity & deterministic stitching
  - Stable user ID, login mapping, device/session stitch, plan/entitlement attributes — ensures correct cohorts and retention analysis.
3. Ingestion pipeline to warehouse & raw export
  - Reliable delivery of raw events to your chosen data store (warehouse or vendor-managed) with durable retention and easy exports.
4. Core dashboards & funnel reports
  - Onboarding funnel, activation cohorts, trial→paid conversion, daily acquisition metrics (MQL -> trial), and feature adoption views.
5. Attribution connectors for ad channels & marketing sources
  - Join ad click/impression data to conversions to measure CPA and channel ROI.
6. Experiment & feature‑flag integration
  - Exposure logs, randomization keys, and A/B attribution so you can measure lift for onboarding and feature experiments.
7. Near‑real‑time feeds / segment exports
  - Webhooks or streaming exports to ESPs, ad platforms, personalization endpoints for retargeting and behavior-triggered messaging.
8. Scheduled exports & data portability
  - SQL access or automated exports (S3, Parquet) so you retain raw data outside the vendor.
9. Alerts, monitoring & data quality checks
  - Data completeness alerts, schema drift detection, and pipeline health dashboards.
10. Advanced analytics features (optional)
  - Predictive scoring, propensity models, or recommendations — only after stable data and initial impact validation.

Success criteria / KPIs to validate pilot (use these to decide whether to extend)
- Data completeness: ≥95% of critical events received and reconciled versus golden source for a sample period.  
- Latency: near‑real‑time events available within chosen SLA (recommended: median <1 min, 95th percentile <5–10 mins for real‑time paths; if not required, <1 hour for batch).  
- Dashboard readiness: core funnels and attribution dashboards populated and accurate within 24–48 hrs of event generation.  
- Identity accuracy: match rate for logged-in users above a threshold (e.g., ≥90% deterministic matches).  
- Experiment attribution: end‑to‑end traceability for at least one A/B test/feature-flag exposure.  
- Reliability: ingestion uptime consistent with SLA (e.g., 99.9% or as negotiated).  
- Business validation: first pilot metric impact visible (e.g., ability to measure Day‑7 activation lift from onboarding changes).

Roles & responsibilities (who does what)
- Vendor provides:
  - Implementation lead/CSM, recommended event taxonomy templates, connectors, dashboards, training, and professional services hours.  
- You provide:
  - Product & PM ownership of event semantics, engineering resources to instrument events and integrate SDKs/webhooks, access to ad/marketing accounts, & legal/compliance info.  
- Collaborative tasks:
  - QA and validation of events, dashboards, and experiment attribution; weekly or biweekly syncs during onboarding.

Risk mitigations & contract protections
- Data portability clause (full raw export).  
- Short pilot with clear exit terms.  
- SLAs tied to performance credits.  
- Security/compliance attestations (SOC2) and right to audit.  
- Avoid exclusive proprietary transforms that lock you in — require standard SQL access or exportable tables.  
- Include a remediation SLA for data quality issues (vendor fixes within defined windows).

Operational considerations & quick wins
- Start by instrumenting the small set of events that feed onboarding, activation, feature usage, and revenue — don’t instrument everything at once.  
- Ask vendor for prebuilt connectors to your ad platforms and ESP to speed attribution and activation.  
- Insist on a short “first 30 days” success milestone (basic ingestion + dashboards) so you can verify momentum early.  
- Map vendor onboarding windows to marketing/launch gates: core ingestion and dashboarding should be complete before marketing ramp (M4), and experiment wiring before Feature A launch (M6).

If you want, I can produce a one‑page SOW template (milestones + deliverables) you can hand to procurement or draft a checklist for the first 6 weeks to track vendor progress. Which would be most useful next?

user: Schedule customer research and beta testing phases: when do we recruit beta users and how many touchpoints should we plan during testing? | assistant: Below is a practical, timeline‑aligned plan for customer research and beta testing for both Feature A (M6 launch) and Feature B (M11 launch), plus recruitment guidance, segmenting, touchpoint cadence, data collection, incentives, and success gating.

High‑level timing mapped to your plan
- Preparatory recruiting window for each feature should begin ~6–8 weeks before external beta.
- Feature A (launch M6)
  - Start recruiting: Month 4 (sprint 7) — build candidate list & screen.
  - Internal alpha / power‑user tests: Month 5 (early sprints 9–10).
  - External beta window: Month 5 → Month 6 (2–4 weeks running into stabilization sprint 12).
- Feature B (launch M11)
  - Start recruiting: Month 8 (sprint 13) — build and screen list.
  - Internal alpha / power‑user tests: Month 9–10 (sprints 17–19).
  - External beta window: Month 10 → Month 11 (4–6 weeks, with staged rollout into sprint 22).

Who to recruit and recommended sample sizes
- Feature A (minimum viable beta)
  - Minimum external beta users: 20 (absolute minimum — aligns with earlier plan). Recommended: 30–50 for stronger signal.
  - Segment targets (example split of 40 users): 50% new/trial users, 30% current power users, 20% lapsed/churned or strategic customers/partners.
  - Why: small qualitative samples (5–10) per segment reveal usability issues; n≈30–50 provides basic quantitative adoption signals.
- Feature B (bigger revenue/monetization feature)
  - Recommended external beta users: 50–100 (larger because monetization signals and retention matter).
  - Segment targets (example split of 80 users): 50% target new cohorts, 30% high‑value/paying customers, 20% partner/integration customers.
  - Why: you’ll want somewhat larger cohorts to measure early revenue impact and retention signals.

Recruitment channels & screening
- Channels: in‑product invitations, email to power users, customer success outreach, partner co‑recruiting, paid sign‑up landing page, and targeted social/LinkedIn outreach.
- Screening criteria: role/industry fit, usage frequency, plan type (free vs paid), prior feedback history, ability & willingness to participate (time commitment), device/browser coverage.
- Incentives: free extended access or paid credits, early‑adopter discounts, gift cards, or recognition (e.g., “founding beta user” status). Make incentives proportional to time/effort.

Touchpoints & cadence (what to plan per beta participant)
Recommended baseline for an external beta that runs 2–6 weeks:
- Pre‑beta (recruit + onboarding)
  - Touchpoint 0: Screening form + confirmation email (1)
  - Touchpoint 1: Onboarding session or short setup guide (Day 0) — 20–30 min (live call or recorded walkthrough)
- Ongoing during beta
  - Touchpoint 2: Day 3 in‑app/check-in email (pulse) — collect initial barriers or friction
  - Touchpoint 3: Day 7 short survey (micro‑survey ~3–5 Qs) + automated telemetry review
  - Touchpoint 4: Mid‑beta qualitative interview (15–30 min, sample subset 8–12 users) — week 2–3
  - Touchpoint 5: Weekly check‑in emails to entire beta pool with issue reporting link + usage prompts
- End of beta
  - Touchpoint 6: End‑of‑beta survey (NPS + task success + feature ratings) — structured
  - Touchpoint 7: Final in‑depth interviews with selected users (15–30 min) to probe motivators, blockers, and willingness to pay
  - Touchpoint 8: Closeout communication + incentive delivery + thank you + next steps
- Ongoing passive data:
  - Continuous telemetry (events, errors, task completions), automated dashboards, and support ticket monitoring throughout the beta.

Number of touchpoints to plan
- Small fast beta (2–3 weeks): plan ~4 scheduled touchpoints per participant (onboarding, Day 3 check, mid short survey, end survey) + 1–2 interviews across a subset.
- Full beta (4–6 weeks): plan ~6–8 touchpoints above (adds weekly check‑ins, mid and final interviews) + continuous telemetry.

Data & feedback capture (mix quantitative + qualitative)
- Quantitative (continuous):
  - Event instrumentation for task completion rates, feature usage frequency, time to first success, errors/exceptions, trial→paid movement.
  - Funnel/cohort dashboards and retention tracking for exposed users.
- Qualitative:
  - Short micro‑surveys (Day 3, Day 7), SUS or task‑success questions, NPS at close.
  - Semi‑structured interviews (mid and end) with recorded notes and thematic analysis.
  - Open bug/feedback channel (Slack channel, dedicated inbox, in‑app feedback widget).
- Bug triage: prioritize P0/P1 and patch during stabilization buffer sprints.

Beta management logistics & roles
- Owner: Product Manager runs recruitment list, script, and prioritization.
- Designer: observes sessions, refines UX, provides fixes.
- Engineering: on‑call during beta, triages issues, ships hotfixes in buffer sprints.
- CS/Operations: handles invitations, scheduling, incentives, and support escalation.
- Marketing: optionally support recruitment and PR for partner beta participants.

Consent, legal & privacy
- Beta consent form or short terms covering data collection, debug access, and NDA if necessary.
- Ensure compliance with GDPR/CCPA for participant data, and provide opt‑out or data deletion options.
- Clear communication about how feedback will be used and whether the beta is representative (not production SLA).

Success criteria to decide go/no‑go or next steps
- Usability / adoption:
  - ≥X% (set target based on baseline) of beta users complete the core task at least once within 7 days (e.g., Feature A target earlier was ≥30% adoption within 60 days; for beta you might expect higher early exposure, e.g., ≥40% within 14 days).
- Quality:
  - Critical bugs: ≤2 P1/P0 issues during beta; otherwise require fix before wide rollout.
- Satisfaction:
  - Median post‑beta task satisfaction ≥4/5 or NPS ≥ +20 among beta users (adjust to company baselines).
- Business signal:
  - Early conversion uplift signal (even small) or clear qualitative evidence the feature meets core needs; if monetization, measurable intent-to-pay signals from high-value users.
- Measurement readiness:
  - Instrumentation validated and end‑to‑end metrics flowing to dashboards.

Staged rollout guidance after beta
- Use feature flags to roll out in cohorts: internal → 5% opt‑in beta users → 25% → 100%, gating each step on bug/error thresholds and adoption/quality metrics.
- Keep stabilization sprint buffer before full launch and reserve engineers on‑call for 48–72 hours after broader rollout.

Practical tips to maximize learnings and minimize friction
- Recruit slightly more participants than target (expect some dropouts). Target 20–30% overrecruit.  
- Prioritize recruiting users who represent the most valuable segments for the feature’s success (power users, new signups, and high‑value customers).  
- Keep surveys short and focused; use interviews to dig deeper.  
- Pair telemetry with qualitative notes to spot mismatches (e.g., users say they saw a problem but telemetry shows they didn’t reach that step).  
- Offer clear, simple ways to report bugs (pre‑filled templates, severity tags) so triage is fast.

- A fillable recruitment script & screener,
- A 6‑week beta calendar template with scheduled touchpoints and owners,
- Or recommended micro‑survey questions and interview guides.

user: Given all of this, what minimal monthly dashboard should we send to the exec team (3-5 core metrics)? | assistant: Keep it tight — one page with 3–5 KPIs, each shown as: current value, MoM delta (%, absolute), sparkline (3–6 months), and status vs target (green/amber/red). Shareable once/month with an owner and one short insight line. Recommended core metrics:

1) Net New MRR (or Net MRR Growth)  
- Definition: New MRR added this month minus MRR lost (churn + downgrades) = net change in MRR.  
- Why: Single best high‑level indicator of top‑line momentum and whether launches/marketing are moving revenue.  
- Target/alert: MoM growth target (set per plan); flag if net MRR < 80% of target or negative for two consecutive months.  
- Owner: Revenue/Finance.  
- Visualization: big number + MoM %, sparkline 6 months, small table showing new MRR / expansion / churn components.

2) Trial → Paid Conversion Rate (30‑day window)  
- Definition: % of new trials in the month that convert to paid within 30 days.  
- Why: Direct proxy for funnel health and marketing efficiency; sensitive to onboarding and feature value.  
- Target/alert: target +X percentage‑points (e.g., +5pp for Q2); flag if conversion falls >10% vs baseline or below target.  
- Owner: Product + Marketing.  
- Visualization: conversion % trend by cohort, conversion funnel numbers (trials → activated → paid).

3) Monthly Churn Rate (customer churn %) or Net Revenue Churn  
- Definition: % of customers (or MRR) lost in the month. Pick customer or revenue churn depending on business model; revenue churn gives dollars view.  
- Why: Retention drives LTV and long‑term profitability; directly tied to the churn‑reduction objective.  
- Target/alert: target reduction trajectory (e.g., −15% vs Q1 by year end); alert if monthly churn spikes >20% vs baseline or shows upward trend 2 months in a row.  
- Owner: Product + CS.  
- Visualization: cohort retention chart (N‑day / N‑month retention) and single‑month churn bar.

4) Activation / Day‑7 Activation Rate  
- Definition: % of new users who reach your defined activation event within 7 days of signup.  
- Why: Early activation predicts conversion and retention; good leading indicator for later metrics.  
- Target/alert: target improvement (e.g., +15% vs baseline); alert if Day‑7 activation drops >10% MoM.  
- Owner: Product + Growth.  
- Visualization: sparkline and cohort comparison (Day‑7 activation by signup week/source).

5) Feature Adoption (Feature A / Feature B adoption %) — optional but recommended around launch windows  
- Definition: % of active users exposed to the feature who use it at least once in X days (e.g., 60 days). Show per‑feature.  
- Why: Measures launch success and product impact (directly tied to objectives).  
- Target/alert: Feature A: ≥30% adoption within 60 days; Feature B: 25–35% adoption within 60 days (adjust to your targets). Flag if adoption <70% of target at the 30‑day checkpoint.  
- Owner: Product.  
- Visualization: adoption curve, usage frequency distribution, and conversion lift vs control (if A/B tests run).

Optional operational health micro‑metric (if you want a 5th replacing feature adoption)
- P1/P0 incidents this month & average MTTR — quick read on technical risk around launches. Flag any P1s during launch windows.

Notes on presentation and actions
- One‑line insight per metric: “Why changed” + “Recommended action” (e.g., “Conversion down 12% — stop new performance spend on Channel X; run onboarding flow A fix”).  
- Use cohort views where possible (by acquisition channel, plan, or signup week) to surface root causes.  
- Decision thresholds: predefine red/amber/green bands for each metric and triggers for immediate follow‑ups (e.g., weekly deep‑dive if any red persists >2 weeks).  
- Data source & freshness: indicate source (analytics vendor / warehouse) and staleness (24h/48h). Execs expect monthly but ensure trending data is available for ad‑hoc questions.

user: Also give me a simple prioritization framework (e.g., effort vs. impact) to help trade off feature work and technical debt decisions. | assistant: Simple prioritization framework — one page you can use in planning sessions

Overview
- Use a consistent scorecard to compare features, experiments, and technical‑debt items across the same dimensions.  
- Combine a numeric rubric with a few absolute gates (safety/compliance/launch blockers) and a capacity rule so you keep space for innovation and debt.

Step 1 — Score each candidate on four dimensions (0–5)
- Business Impact (0–5): revenue, acquisition, retention, upsell potential.  
- User Value / Pain (0–5): how much it improves core user workflows or reduces churn.  
- Strategic Alignment (0–5): fits company priorities (e.g., supports Feature A/B, marketing needs, or long‑term platform goals).  
- Effort (0–5, where 5 = highest effort): developer weeks, QA, docs, integrations.

Optional extra for tech debt items
- Risk Reduction (0–5): how much it lowers outage/incident probability, security exposure, or maintenance cost.

Step 2 — Compute two simple scores
- Priority Score (feature/experiment): Priority = Business Impact + User Value + Strategic Alignment − Effort
- Tech Debt Score (include risk): TechPriority = Risk Reduction + Strategic Alignment + (Business/Operational Impact if applicable) − Effort

Notes on scoring
- All terms 0–5, so Priority ranges −5 → +15. Higher is better.  
- Treat Effort as a subtractive penalty (big, long projects need high compensating impact).  
- For pure experiments, add an “Information Value” bonus (0–3) to Business Impact if the experiment reduces big uncertainty.

Step 3 — Absolute gates (must‑do / must‑not)
- Must do immediately (override scoring):
  - Security/PCI/GDPR compliance fixes, legal/regulatory obligations, critical production incidents, or anything that will materially block a launch (Feature A/B).  
- Must not do:
  - Large projects that fail to meet minimum Impact threshold (e.g., Business Impact + User Value < 4) unless required for compliance or launch.

Step 4 — Prioritization buckets & how to pick
- Quick Wins (fast, high impact): Priority ≥ 8 and Effort ≤ 2 — do first.  
- Strategic Bets (big impact, higher effort): Priority ≥ 8 and Effort ≥ 3 — plan and reserve capacity; break into milestones.  
- Maintain & Fix (operational/low glamour): TechPriority ≥ 6 — schedule in ops sprints or as part of launch hardening.  
- Defer or Kill: Priority < 4 and TechPriority < 4 — deprioritize.

Step 5 — Capacity & allocation rules
- Suggested allocation of engineering capacity as a rule of thumb:
  - 60–70% for committed roadmap (top prioritized features/launch-critical work).  
  - 15–25% for technical debt, reliability, and operations automation.  
  - 10–20% for experiments and exploratory work (new channels, small bets).  
- Don’t commit >80% capacity in a sprint that precedes a major launch; preserve buffer.

Step 6 — Process & cadence
- Monthly triage: PM + Eng leads run the scorecard for new and backlog items; update scores with data (MRR, support volumes, experiment results).  
- Quarterly roadmap review: re-score large items and reassign priorities based on new metrics and hires.  
- Sprint planning: pick from top of the prioritized list until capacity targets (above) are met; ensure at least one tech‑debt ticket per sprint if score warrants.

Quick decision heuristics (practical rules)
- If an item is a launch blocker, it jumps ahead regardless of score.  
- If a tech debt item reduces developer time spent on maintenance by >20% (or saves >X hours/month), prioritize it — quick ROI.  
- If an experiment has low cost (Effort ≤ 1) and moderate potential impact (Impact ≥ 3), run it immediately.  
- Break large high‑effort items into smaller milestones with intermediate value so you can stop if returns are poor.

Example (illustrative)
- Feature X: Business 4 + User 4 + Strategic 3 − Effort 2 = Priority 9 → Quick Win/Do this quarter.  
- Refactor Y (tech debt): Risk 4 + Strategic 2 − Effort 3 = TechPriority 3 → low priority unless it blocks release or maintenance pain > threshold.

How to use this in practice (one‑page checklist)
1. Score each candidate quickly (5–10 min) using available data.  
2. Apply absolute gates.  
3. Bucket and allocate capacity.  
4. Publish the selected list with owners and expected sprint(s).  
5. Revisit scores monthly with real metrics (conversion lift, support hours, MRR effects).

If you want, I can produce a simple spreadsheet template with the score fields, auto‑calculated Priority/TechPriority, and suggested bucket coloring so you can copy/paste into your planning tool.

user: Define decision checkpoints every quarter: what triggers a pivot and what data should we require to change course? | assistant: Quarterly decision‑checkpoint framework — when to pivot, and the data required to change course

Purpose
- Give a repeatable, lightweight governance cadence so you can stop wasting time on losing bets and reallocate resources quickly when signals require a course change.

Cadence
- Quarterly checkpoints at the end of each quarter (formal review), plus fast‑track reviews anytime a “trigger” threshold is hit (see emergency triggers).  
- Typical timeline: 1–2 day prework to validate data, 1 full checkpoint meeting (2 hours), decisions captured and actioned within 1–4 weeks depending on scope.

Checkpoint template (one page to fill each quarter)
- Review period: last 90 days (with rolling 30/60/90 views)  
- Owner(s): Head of Product + Head of Marketing + Head of Engineering + Finance rep  
- Core KPIs reviewed (examples): Net New MRR, Trial→Paid conversion (30d), Monthly churn (revenue & customers), Day‑7 activation, Feature adoption (A/B cohorts), CAC & LTV estimate, Ops automation ROI, hiring status & time‑to‑ramp.  
- Decision options: Continue as planned, Turbo (scale), Reallocate (shift spend/heads), Pivot (re‑scope roadmap/markets), Pause/Stop (defer launch/kill work), Escalate to board (if material to runway or strategy).  
- Required sign‑offs: Exec team for tactical pivots; board approval if >20% budget reallocation or >1 quarter delay to major launch.

Triggers that should prompt a pivot (with thresholds) and required evidence
1) Revenue underperformance (acquisition/monetization)
- Trigger:
  - Net New MRR <80% of quarterly plan target for the quarter OR
  - Two consecutive months of negative net MRR growth.
- Required data:
  - MRR decomposition: new MRR / expansion / churn details, cohort performance (by signup week and major channel).  
  - Channel-level CAC and conversion trends (last 30/90 days).  
  - Attribution for recent marketing spend and feature launches.
- Typical pivot actions:
  - Reallocate marketing budget away from underperforming channels; reduce marketing spend; or double down on high-performing cohorts. Consider re‑pricing or limited promotions.
- Decision speed: tactical reallocation within 1 week; strategic pivot (e.g., new GTM) within 4 weeks after deeper analysis.

2) Funnel & conversion collapse (growth signal)
- Trigger:
  - Trial→Paid conversion (30d) drops >10% relative or below minimum target threshold for the quarter.  
  - Day‑7 activation drops >10% vs baseline.
- Required data:
  - Funnel by cohort and source, landing page and campaign performance, onboarding telemetry (event coverage), A/B experiment outcomes (with p‑values).  
  - Qualitative feedback from recent beta users / CS tickets.
- Typical pivot actions:
  - Pause scaling spend until fixes; prioritize onboarding bug fixes/flow changes; run rapid experiments; reassign engineering capacity to must‑have onboarding fixes.
- Decision speed: immediate (1–2 week fixes) for tactical changes; larger product changes may require reprioritization across next sprint.

3) Retention / churn deterioration
- Trigger:
  - Monthly churn increases >15% relative vs Q1 baseline OR sustained upward trend over 2 quarters.  
  - Significant retention drop in newly acquired cohorts post‑launch.
- Required data:
  - Cohort retention curves (30/60/90), churn reasons (CS tickets, surveys), ARR at risk, NPS trends.  
  - Impact of recent releases on retention cohorts (segmented by exposure to features).
- Typical pivot actions:
  - Halt aggressive acquisition, reassign CS and product to retention initiatives, accelerate ops automations that directly affect churn (billing, onboarding).  
- Decision speed: escalate to exec for resource reallocation within 2 weeks.

4) Feature launch failure / adoption shortfall
- Trigger:
  - Feature adoption at 30‑day checkpoint <70% of target for that milestone OR <threshold of core KPI improvement (e.g., conversion lift not observed in exposed cohort).  
  - >3 P1 production incidents attributable to feature in first 30 days.
- Required data:
  - Adoption metrics by cohort, experiment/control comparisons with statistical significance, support/bug volume, performance/error telemetry, customer qualitative interviews.  
  - Rollout logs and exposure sampling.
- Typical pivot actions:
  - Pause broader rollout, roll back or hotfix, re-scope feature to MVP and re-run beta tests. Reprioritize engineering for stability.  
- Decision speed: immediate staged rollback if P1s; otherwise 1–2 week stabilization plan and re-assessment.

5) Hiring & capacity risk
- Trigger:
  - Critical hire delayed >4 weeks from planned start OR a key departure that creates >4 weeks of gap in critical-path work.  
  - Ramp metrics show new hire not achieving contribution in expected 90 days.
- Required data:
  - Hiring pipeline (candidates/interviews), contractor availability & cost, sprint velocity impact simulation, backlog burn rate.  
- Typical pivot actions:
  - Engage contractors for short blocks, re-scope feature to MVP, freeze nonessential work, accelerate recruiting spend (referrals/agency).  
- Decision speed: implement contractor hires within 1–2 weeks; decide on launch risk within 2 weeks.

6) Vendor or data reliability issues
- Trigger:
  - Analytics vendor or payment processor SLA breach > agreed threshold OR persistent data quality problems that affect decisioning.  
- Required data:
  - Vendor incident reports, data completeness/loss metrics, reconciliation reports, alternative vendor feasibility.  
- Typical pivot actions:
  - Failover to backup flows, pause data‑driven campaigns until validated, use manual reconciliation for billing temporarily, engage vendor remediation clauses.  
- Decision speed: immediate operational mitigations; vendor contract escalation within 48–72 hours.

7) Financial runway & budget triggers
- Trigger:
  - Runway falls below a pre‑set threshold (e.g., <6 months) OR burn rate exceeds plan by >15% for a quarter.  
- Required data:
  - Cash forecast, updated burn vs plan, spend-to-date by bucket (R&D/Marketing/Ops).  
- Typical pivot actions:
  - Immediate cost reductions (pause hiring, cut nonessential spend), seek bridge funding, prioritize revenue-driving initiatives only.  
- Decision speed: executive + board notification within 48 hours; actions implemented within 7 days.

Data quality & statistical requirements before pivoting
- Require validated data from primary sources (analytics vendor / warehouse) and at least one reconciled cross‑check (e.g., product event counts vs backend logs; revenue vs billing ledger).  
- For experiments: require p ≤ 0.05 and power ≥80% to treat lift as statistically significant for scaling decisions. If sample sizes are small, treat signals as directional and run extended tests rather than full pivots.  
- Provide cohort windows (30/60/90 days) and sample sizes in the checkpoint pack; annotate limitations.

Decision process & ownership
- Pre‑work: KPI owners prepare a 1‑page factsheet (metrics, trends, suggested action) 3 business days before the checkpoint.  
- Meeting: Exec checkpoint meeting (Product, Marketing, Eng, Finance + CEO). 2 hour max. Review factsheets, run through triggers.  
- Decision: Assign action owner(s), timeline (immediate/tactical within 1–2 weeks; strategic within 4 weeks), and success metrics for the pivot.  
- Escalation: If the pivot affects cash/runway materially (>10% budget reallocation) or the product roadmap by >1 quarter, escalate to Board for approval within 7 business days.

Types of pivots (clear options to pick)
- Scale up / Turbo: increase spend or headcount on a winning bet. Data required: sustained KPI outperformance vs plan and positive LTV:CAC trajectory.  
- Reallocate: move resources from underperforming channels/features into higher-performing ones. Data required: channel ROI, feature impact analysis.  
- Re‑scope: cut noncritical feature scope to meet launch dates. Data required: impact analysis showing minimal revenue loss and preserved measurement.  
- Pause / Stop: stop a feature or campaign if it damages core metrics. Data required: statistical evidence of harm or material negative financial impact.  
- Delay / Stage: proceed with a limited cohort rollout and re-evaluate. Data required: sample-based results and stabilization plan.

Reporting & follow up
- After decision, publish a 1‑page decision log: what changed, why, who owns it, what metrics will prove success and by when.  
- Weekly tactical check‑ins until the pivot is stabilized (owner reports progress weekly).  
- At next quarterly checkpoint, include a short retrospective on pivot outcomes (did it meet targets?).

Practical thresholds summary (quick reference)
- Net MRR: <80% of plan → review; negative for 2 months → pivot.  
- Trial→Paid conversion: drop >10% → pause spend + triage.  
- Churn: increase >15% vs baseline → prioritize retention fixes.  
- Feature adoption: <70% of target at 30d → pause rollout & fix.  
- Hiring gap: critical hire delayed >4 weeks → bring contractor + re-scope.  
- Runway: <6 months remaining → immediate cost actions & board notification.

Final tips
- Always pair quantitative triggers with qualitative signals (CS feedback, sales pipeline commentary, partner input).  
- Avoid knee‑jerk pivots on noisy short‑term blips; require persistence or statistical support unless the trigger is safety/compliance.  
- Make pivots time‑boxed and measurable — define the success bar before reallocating.
